title: Mourning Rage: The Year After
dek: When the fire finally burns out, what remains is not peace, but a vibrating emptiness. Here is how the body learns to hold the grief that follows the fury, muscle by muscle, breath by breath.
bodyMarkdown:
## Mourning Rage: The Year After

You wake in the quiet hours, that strange time just before dawn when the world is still and your body is loud. The masseter muscle, that fierce clencher along your jawline, pulses with a memory you thought had passed. It’s not pain exactly, but a hollow sort of vibration—like the echo of a storm retreating, leaving the air charged and still. This is the somatic aftermath of rage, the body’s own haunted terrain in the year after the fire has gone out.

Rage is not a moment but a tidal wave. It rises in your chest, a tightening of the diaphragm, an accordion of breath squeezed into the narrowest spaces. It roars through the trapezius and levator scapulae, those traps along your neck and shoulders that bear the burden of your fury like silent sentinels. But when the wave recedes, what remains is often a body emptied yet vibrating, a psoas muscle that no longer coils with fury but trembles with absence. You are mourning the rage that once was your fierce companion.

## The Body Remembers What the Mind Forgets

The year after rage is a strange, liminal space. You are neither the storm nor the calm. Your body holds onto the shape of your fury—the way your masseter clenched so hard the fascia beneath your skin thickened, the way your psoas contracted as if bracing for impact, your breath caught high in your diaphragm. You try to tell yourself the rage is gone, that you’ve moved on. But the body remembers. It’s not pathology; this is biology.

Thich Nhat Hanh taught that “the body knows” before the mind understands. You can try to intellectualize your rage away, but the muscles keep score. The masseter, that relentless grinder of teeth, still tightens in moments of quiet, as if ready to defend against an unseen threat. Your psoas—deep and primal—twitches with the memory of tension, a muscle that holds your fight-or-flight like a secret. This is the body’s ledger, the somatic archive of rage.

## The Hollow Vibrancy of Aftermath

There is a peculiar sensation in the aftermath of rage that defies easy description. It is an emptiness that feels almost full—like a room cleared of furniture but still humming with the memory of movement. Your diaphragm, usually the bellows of breath, sometimes flutters as if unsure how to fill the space left by rage’s departure. The levator scapulae and trapezius relax, but not fully. They hold a residual tension, a quiet readiness that feels like waiting for the next storm.

Your skin smells faintly of yourself, the musk of adrenaline and sweat now faded into a subtle, almost imperceptible note. It is the scent of survival, not shame. This is not a failure of character but the body’s way of mourning what was once a vital force. Rage is a messenger, and when it leaves, its absence is a message of its own. You grieve the loss of that fierce clarity, that edge of protection, that undeniable presence in the world.

## Mourning the Rage That Was

Mourning rage is not about regret or guilt. It is about acknowledging the deep, embodied loss of a power you once wielded, even if it felt dangerous or overwhelming. Robert Augustus Masters reminds us that emotions are not enemies but allies, and when rage fades, its departure leaves a vacuum that the body instinctively knows. Your psoas muscle, that deep core stabilizer, may feel unmoored, trembling in the stillness where once it was tightly coiled.

This mourning is a slow unfolding. You notice how your masseter no longer clenches with the same ferocity but sometimes spasms, a muscle remembering how to hold rage even when you are trying to let it go. Your trapezius may ache in quiet moments, a reminder that rage was once your armor. The body’s fascia, that connective tissue web, remembers tension like a map, and the pathways of rage linger beneath your skin.

## The Year After: A Somatic Landscape

In the year after, your body is a landscape marked by the traces of rage. You can feel it in the subtle shift of your breath, the way your diaphragm sometimes catches in shallow, uncertain rhythms. Your shoulders may slump in moments of exhaustion, your levator scapulae quietly protesting the loss of the fight it once fueled. The psoas, that ancient muscle tied to survival and grounding, may twitch with a hollow vibrancy, a body still searching for its footing.

This is not a sign of weakness but of profound change. You are learning to inhabit a body that once burned with rage and now pulses with the quiet ache of mourning. The body keeps its own time, and this year after is a somatic reckoning. It asks you to listen—to the subtle vibrations in your masseter, the deep tremors in your psoas, the gentle sighs of your diaphragm. The rage may have passed, but its echo lingers, and in that echo, there is a story your body is still telling.

## The Long Haul of Mourning Rage

You wake at 3:17 a.m., the cold night air pressing through the cracked window, carrying the faint metallic scent of rain on concrete. Your trapezius muscles knot tight like a noose, the levator scapulae refusing to soften despite your attempts to breathe. This is no ordinary insomnia; it’s the body's echo chamber of grief, reverberating with the unspent fury lodged deep in your masseter, clenched so relentlessly that your teeth ache with a dull, persistent throb. The year after—the body remembers. Not just the loss, but the rage that swells beneath your ribs, tightening your diaphragm, making each inhale a protest.

Karla McLaren, whose work on anger reframes fury as a messenger rather than a monster, offers a profound lens here. She teaches that anger is not a mere byproduct of grief but an integral part of it—a dynamic force that insists on acknowledgment. When you mourn, anger is the fierce guardian of your boundaries, the body's way of saying, “This is not acceptable.” It’s a protector of the vulnerable heart beneath the rawness. But if you were taught anger was ugly, unlovable, or dangerous—if your culture or family painted fury as a moral failing—then your body learns to hide it, to stuff it deep into the fascia and the muscle fibers where it festers and distorts.

The biology of this aftermath is not metaphorical; it’s chemical and cellular. The adrenals, those tiny but mighty glands perched atop your kidneys, flood your bloodstream with cortisol and adrenaline during the initial trauma. Months later, when the shock should have waned, these stress hormones sometimes remain elevated, a constant simmer that primes your nervous system into a state of hypervigilance. You may feel your psoas muscle—the deep core stabilizer—locked into a protective spasm, shortening with each breath in an unconscious attempt to brace against further emotional assault. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is telling you, “Danger still lives here.”

## The Jaw Remembers What the Heart Forgets

The jaw, that complex architecture of bone and muscle—the masseter, temporalis, and pterygoids—has a memory all its own. When you were told to “be quiet” or “don’t make a scene,” your jaw clenched in silent rebellion. Karla McLaren points out that anger’s role is to clarify: it identifies what has been violated, what needs protecting. In the quiet moments of mourning, the jaw may tighten reflexively, a somatic bookmark of your internal struggle.

You might notice this in the way your teeth grind during the dead hours before dawn, or how your tongue presses against the roof of your mouth, a subtle but constant tension. This is your body’s way of holding the line, even when your mind wants to collapse into exhaustion. It is not weakness; it is survival.

McLaren’s approach encourages you to listen to this anger with curiosity, not judgment. When you feel that surge beneath your ribs or the grinding in your jaw, instead of shaming yourself for “losing it” or “being too much,” you begin to ask: What boundary has been crossed? What truth refuses to be silenced? This is the work of mourning rage—not to be tamed or denied, but to be understood as a vital, living response to loss.

## The Aftershock in the Fascia

Bessel van der Kolk, whose research on trauma illuminates the body’s deep imprint of pain, reminds us that trauma lodges itself in the fascia—the connective tissue wrapping every muscle, nerve, and organ. This is why the ache in your neck or the stiffness in your shoulders can feel as stubborn as the grief itself. The fascia, dense and unyielding, remembers the emotional tremors that your conscious mind might try to push away.

In this year after, your body may resist relaxation because the fascia is still bracing for impact. The levator scapulae, that thin muscle running from the upper cervical vertebrae to the shoulder blade, often seizes in response to ongoing stress, creating a “brace” that feels like a permanent shrug of burden. This physical tension is your body’s way of holding space for your anger and loss—an unspoken memorial in muscle and tissue.

Herbs can be potent allies in this process. A tincture of [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) or a decoction of [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) may ease the nervous system’s overdrive, while topical applications of [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) or a warm compress infused with [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) can soften the fascia’s grip. These are not magic bullets but respectful invitations to the body: “It’s safe now. You can let go.”

## The Year’s Quiet Revolutions

At dusk, when the light fades to that peculiar shade of bruised purple, you might catch yourself breathing differently. Perhaps the diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, begins to soften its hold. This muscle, so crucial for life and emotional expression, often gets locked tight in grief and rage. When you allow yourself even a moment of full breath, you are signaling to your nervous system that you are still here, still fighting, still surviving.

Pema Chödrön’s teachings on leaning into discomfort resonate here. She urges us to stay present with our pain, to meet it “like a good friend,” no matter how fierce or unwelcome the emotions. Anger, in this frame, is not an enemy but a guide through the dark corridors of loss.

The year after mourning rage is not linear. There are nights when the fury roars, muscles trembling under the weight of it, and others when it recedes just enough to catch a fragile breath. This is the body’s slow alchemy—turning pain into presence, rage into resilience.

You are not broken. The jaw remembers, the fascia holds, the adrenals guard fiercely. This is not a curse but a testament to your survival. Karla McLaren would say: honor the messenger. Listen to the body’s language of rage and grief, because in that fierce dialogue lies the possibility of wholeness, one breath at a time.

## The Body Registers What the Mind Denies

You don’t just feel the year after grief in your heart or your mind; you feel it in the spaces between your ribs, in the tight coil of your psoas, in the tremor beneath your jaw’s masseter muscle. Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work *The Body Keeps the Score*, reminds us that trauma—mourning’s close companion—lodges itself in the body’s tissues long after the mind tries to move on. The body is a somber historian, etching the story of what you’ve lost into fascia and muscle, into the rhythm of your breath and the cadence of your heartbeat. The year after grief is not a timeline of neat closure. Instead, it is a slow, often jagged process of the body slowly releasing what it has been holding onto, sometimes in ways you don’t even recognize.

Van der Kolk describes how trauma rewires the nervous system, locking you into states of hypervigilance or numbness. You might notice your trapezius muscles—those broad, wing-like bands running from your neck to your shoulders—chronically tense by mid-afternoon, a silent testament to the stress that your mind refuses to acknowledge. Your diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, may stiffen, making deep breaths feel like a distant memory. This is not simply “stress”; it is the body’s attempt to keep you alive, to keep you safe, even when safety feels elusive. Mourning rage is encoded in these patterns. The rage that wells up unexpectedly is not a failure of your character but a biological echo of the body desperately trying to recalibrate.

## The Nervous System’s Wilderness and Herbal Allies

Supporting the nervous system during this turbulent year is less about forcing calm and more about gently inviting the body to remember its own capacity for peace. The herbs you use are allies in this somatic conversation, not quick fixes or magic bullets. Valerian root, for example, is a potent nervine that soothes the diaphragm and calms the overstimulated vagus nerve, which governs the parasympathetic response. Taken as a tincture or a slow, contemplative tea in the early evening, valerian can ease the tightness in your ribcage that holds your grief. A product like [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a concentrated form that respects the herb's earthy, musky qualities.

Another ally is skullcap, a herb that works on the levator scapulae—the small muscles at the back and side of your neck that often lock up when the body senses danger. Skullcap’s gentle sedative properties encourage these muscles to release their grip, reducing that brittle, brittle tension that can erupt into sudden tears or sharp bursts of anger. A tincture such as [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be taken throughout the day in measured doses, especially when you notice the familiar knot forming at the base of your skull.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), the liver is understood as the organ responsible for the smooth flow of qi, or life energy, and is intimately connected to the regulation of anger. When grief lodges itself in the body, it often disrupts the liver’s rhythm, leading to what TCM calls “liver qi stagnation.” Herbs like bupleurum and peony root support this flow, encouraging the body to process both the emotional and physical residue of mourning. While these herbs require careful preparation, companies like [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) offer high-quality tinctures that are crafted with an understanding of this ancient wisdom, allowing you to support your body’s internal rhythms with precision.

## Night’s Quiet Witness and the Smell of Healing

In the late hours, when the world outside is softened by darkness and the air is cool against your skin, you might notice the way your body remembers rage differently. The sharpness in your trapezius mellows, the jaw’s clench loosens slightly, but the grief still hums like a low drum beneath your ribs. This is when the body’s memory is loudest because the distractions of day have fallen away. Your breath, perhaps still shallow, carries the scent of herbs you’ve taken—valerian’s earthy musk, the faint floral bitterness of skullcap, the green, slightly anise-like note of bupleurum. This is not a magic spell but a slow conversation between your nervous system and these plant allies.

You may find yourself sitting with a cup of [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20), its gentle warmth spreading through your chest, coaxing the diaphragm to relax just a fraction more. The ritual of preparing the tea, the smell of the steam rising, the heat against your palms—these are somatic anchors. They remind the body that it is safe to feel the ache, safe to mourn the rage that has passed, safe to be exactly where you are in this long, quiet year after.

## The Final Exhale

The year after the fire burns out is not a time of neat resolutions or tidy endings. It is a slow, somatic unspooling, a process of learning to inhabit a body that has been irrevocably changed by the heat of its own fury. The masseter muscle, once locked in a perpetual clench, begins to soften, though it may still twitch in the quiet hours before dawn. The psoas, that deep core stabilizer that held your fight-or-flight response like a coiled spring, slowly learns to release its grip, allowing your pelvis to settle and your breath to deepen. This is not a return to who you were before the rage; it is an arrival at who you are now.

You are a body that has survived its own storm. The trapezius and levator scapulae, those sentinels of tension along your neck and shoulders, may still ache on damp, cold mornings, but the ache is different now. It is less a bracing against the world and more a quiet acknowledgment of the weight you have carried. The diaphragm, once tight and restricted, begins to expand with a new rhythm, drawing in air that smells of rain and damp earth, of [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20), herbs that have walked with you through the darkest parts of this year.

## An Embodied Practice for the Year After

When the hollow vibrancy of the aftermath feels too loud, when the body vibrates with the memory of rage but the fire is gone, try this simple practice. Find a quiet space where you will not be disturbed. Sit or lie down in a position that feels supportive to your spine. Place one hand over your heart and the other over your belly, just below the navel. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or soften your gaze on a spot in front of you.

Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice if the masseter is clenched. Without forcing it, invite the lower jaw to drop slightly, creating space between your teeth. Let your tongue rest gently on the roof of your mouth, just behind your front teeth. Now, shift your focus to your breath. Feel the rise and fall of your belly beneath your hand. As you inhale, imagine the breath traveling down into the deep bowl of your pelvis, gently massaging the psoas. As you exhale, imagine the breath traveling up and out, carrying with it any residual tension from the trapezius and levator scapulae.

Repeat this for five to ten breaths. If your mind wanders, gently bring it back to the sensation of your hands on your body, the subtle movement of your breath, the softening of your jaw. This is not about achieving a state of perfect calm; it is about offering your body a moment of compassionate witness. It is about saying to your nervous system, "I am here. We are safe."

## FAQ: Navigating the Somatic Aftermath

**Q: Why do I still feel so exhausted even though the intense anger has passed?**

**A:** The body requires an immense amount of energy to sustain the physiological state of rage. The adrenals pump out cortisol and adrenaline, the muscles brace for impact, the heart rate elevates. When the rage finally subsides, the body experiences a profound energetic crash. This exhaustion is not a sign of weakness; it is the body's biological imperative to rest and repair. The fascia, the muscles, the nervous system—they all need time to recalibrate. Honor this exhaustion. It is the body's way of demanding the rest it was denied during the storm.

**Q: My jaw still clenches at night, even when I feel relatively calm during the day. Is this normal?**

**A:** Yes, it is entirely normal. The masseter muscle is one of the strongest muscles in the body and is deeply connected to our primal survival responses. During the day, your conscious mind may be able to override the body's instinct to brace, but at night, when the conscious mind rests, the body's deeper somatic memories surface. The jaw clenching is a residual echo of the rage, a physical manifestation of the boundaries you had to fiercely protect. Gentle massage, warm compresses, and nervine herbs like [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe this nocturnal tension.

**Q: How do I know if I am mourning the rage or just stuck in it?**

**A:** Mourning rage feels different in the body than being stuck in it. When you are stuck in rage, the body feels tight, hot, and coiled, ready to strike. The breath is shallow, the heart rate is elevated, the muscles are braced. Mourning rage, on the other hand, often feels like a hollow vibrancy, an emptiness that vibrates with the memory of the fire. There may be sadness, exhaustion, and a sense of profound loss. The body feels less like a weapon and more like a landscape that has been scorched but is beginning to heal. If you are unsure, pay attention to your breath. If you can find moments of deep, expansive breathing, you are likely in the process of mourning.

**Q: Can herbs really help with this somatic grief?**

**A:** Yes, herbs can be profound allies in the somatic processing of grief and rage. They do not "cure" the emotion, but they support the physical structures—the nervous system, the fascia, the organs—that bear the brunt of the emotional weight. Nervines like [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Doctor's Best High Absorption Magnesium Glycinate, 240 Count](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000BD0RT0?tag=spankyspinola-20) help to soothe an overactive nervous system, while adaptogens like [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (1 lb)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0058AADU4?tag=spankyspinola-20) support the adrenals in recovering from prolonged stress. Herbs offer a gentle, biological intervention that respects the body's innate wisdom and capacity for healing.
